focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Cora had washed the oilcloth twice and it still held a gray bloom in the cracks, the table keeping faith with the weather instead of with her. She stood in the kitchen with the dishrag twisted tight between both hands and looked at the room August Poeschel had decided a federal man should see: stove blacked as well as cheap polish allowed, flour-sack curtains beaten and rehung, cups turned so the worst chips faced the wall, the enamel basin set mouth-down to hide the rust along the rim. None of it improved the house. It only showed that she had spent the morning trying.

August's note lay under the sugar jar where she had shoved it after breakfast. Mr. Walter Knapp of the resettlement office would call at eleven. Kindly remain on the premises. Kindly had been the word that raised the heat in her, more even than office. There was nowhere else for her to go at eleven on a Wednesday except the farm she was being asked to watch die in good order.

The wind was up enough to rasp the screen door, not enough yet to make a storm of itself. Dust moved low over the yard in nervous little drifts that gathered at the porch steps and broke apart. Out by the pump Dell had the hood off the truck again, though truck was grander than what sat there: a Ford with one fender tied on by wire and a temperament meaner than a kicked mule. He leaned in with a wrench in his fist, shirt stuck dark between his shoulders, wrists all bone and tendon. He had come in late the night before with damp earth on his hands and some tight silence he had not yet spent. When he straightened he looked older from the back than he ever did from the front.

"You want the hood down before he gets here?" he called.

"Leave it."

He wiped his forearm over his face and left a gray streak across his cheek....
prev_tail_excerpt:
...e washstand. "If he'd come in for water."

Cora saw it at once. She saw Dell half-awake, thirsty, pushing the door with his shoulder. She saw morning light on the bowl before she had wiped it. She saw any pair of eyes in any house where dust settled after a storm and nobody could stop the weather from keeping receipts. The thought was not grand. It was domestic, and that made it worse.

"He didn't," Cora said.

"No." Thea swallowed. Her voice came back with effort. "But if I stay, that's part of what staying is."

Cora turned then. Thea stood naked except for the one stocking still around her ankle, hair down, letter on the washstand behind her, the mark of Cora's mouth still visible at her collarbone. There was no decent lie left in the room. "Yes," Cora said.

Thea looked at her for a long beat, measuring the basin, the wall, the door, the letter, the bed too near the wall and the town beyond it. Then she reached back without looking, took the folded Lincoln letter from beside the lamp, and slid it under Cora's brush instead of into her dress.

"I'll wire them Monday for another day," she said. "I'm not promising you harvest in the dark. But I'm not walking into Lincoln Tuesday as if this room doesn't change anything. If you still mean it when the sun's up, say it then. Until Lincoln answers, I'm here."

It was a stay with terms and a date on it, only a few days bought against Lincoln and daylight, but chosen all the same.

When Thea came back to the bed, she crossed the room stripped of caution by the enamel bowl, by Dell breathing on the far side of the wall, by Lincoln folded under the brush, by Monday already pressing on them. Cora knew the night had turned into something riskier and more binding than comfort, the sort of thing daylight would make them answer for.
